content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To     :  File                                          Date  :  Oct. 25, 1976


From  :  FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Ken Finney

Subject:  Telephone Call from Ross Mumphrey


         Ross Mumphrey of Senator Rains's office called today
to inquire about the potential reporting obligations
which would attach under the following circumstances.
The Senator has drafted a letter to the editor stating
his position on a statewide ballot measure and intends
to send the letter on legislative stationery.  He in-
tends to send approximately 290 copies of the letter to
various newspapers.  The Senator is not a candidate for
reelection this year and thus is not subject to the pro-
hibitions of Section 89001.  Accordingly, the only issue
under the Political Reform Act is whether the letter to
the editor somehow would constitute a campaign expendi-
ture or contribution to the committee advocating the po-
sition with which the Senator agrees.  Mr. Mumphrey in-
dicated that the amount of any expenditure would be less
than $500.00 and, thus, I stated that the Senator would
not become a Section 82013(b) committee by reason of the
sending of the letter.  I indicated, however, that if
the letter were sent at the behest of a campaign commit-
tee, it could constitute an in-kind contribution to the
campaign committee which would be reportable by that com-
mittee.  I explained to Mr. Mumphrey that at the behest
means under the direction or control of the campaign com-
mittee.  I also stated that whether or not an expenditure
of public funds can constitute a campaign contribution
to a committee is a close question, but that the Commis-
sion has concluded that there definitely are circumstances
under which such an expenditure will constitute a report-
able contribution.

         Mr. Mumphrey indicated that the letter was not being pre-
pared under the direction or control of a campaign committee.
He stated that the Senator was drafting the letter
himself.  Under these circumstances, I stated that the
letter would not constitute a campaign contribution and,
thus, the issue of whether the expenditure of public funds
could constitute a reportable contribution is avoided.

Memorandum to File - Ross Humphrey (Senator Rains) call.
Page Two
October 25, 1976

Finally, I indicated to Mr. Humphrey that whether an expendi-
ture of public funds to advocate a position on a ballot mea-
sure could constitute a waste of public funds was not an issue
which is within the jurisdiction of the Fair Political Practices
Commission. I stated that the Attorney General has primary
responsibility for the enforcement of statutory provisions
involving a waste of public funds and suggested that, if he
wanted to obtain an opinion about the legality of such an
expenditure, he should contact the Attorney General.


KF:jo

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a state senator's mailing of approximately 290 copies of a letter to the editor expressing his position on a statewide ballot measure, sent on legislative stationery, would constitute a reportable campaign expenditure or contribution under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The letter would not constitute a campaign contribution or expenditure triggering reporting requirements because it was drafted by the Senator himself rather than at the behest of a campaign committee. The question of whether expenditure of public funds could constitute a waste of public funds is outside FPPC jurisdiction and should be directed to the Attorney General.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal memorandum to file documenting phone advice given by Ken Finney to Ross Mumphrey regarding Senator Rains's letter mailing. No formal Q&A sections present; structured as narrative memo. Confidence reduced slightly due to lack of explicit conclusion section, though the advice is clearly stated in the body text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a state senator's mailing of approximately 290 copies of a letter to the editor expressing his position on a statewide ballot measure, sent on legislative stationery, would constitute a reportable campaign expenditure or contribution under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The letter would not constitute a campaign contribution or expenditure triggering reporting requirements because it was drafted by the Senator himself rather than at the behest of a campaign committee. The question of whether expenditure of public funds could constitute a waste of public funds is outside FPPC jurisdiction and should be directed to the Attorney General.

The Senator has drafted a letter to the editor stating his position on a statewide ballot measure and intends to send the letter on legislative stationery. He in- tends to send approximately 290 copies of the letter to various newspapers. Accordingly, the only issue under the Political Reform Act is whether the letter to the editor somehow would constitute a campaign expendi- ture or contribution to the committee advocating the po- sition with which the Senator agrees. Mr. Mumphrey in- dicated that the amount of any expenditure would be less than $500.00 and, thus, I stated that the Senator would not become a Section 82013(b) committee by reason of the sending of the letter. I indicated, however, that if the letter were sent at the behest of a campaign commit- tee, it could constitute an in-kind contribution to the campaign committee which would be reportable by that com- mittee. I explained to Mr. Mumphrey that at the behest means under the direction or control of the campaign com- mittee. Mr. Mumphrey indicated that the letter was not being pre- pared under the direction or control of a campaign committee. He stated that the Senator was drafting the letter himself. Under these circumstances, I stated that the letter would not constitute a campaign contribution and, thus, the issue of whether the expenditure of public funds could constitute a reportable contribution is avoided. Memorandum to File - Ross Humphrey (Senator Rains) call. Page Two October 25, 1976 Finally, I indicated to Mr. Humphrey that whether an expendi- ture of public funds to advocate a position on a ballot mea- sure could constitute a waste of public funds was not an issue which is within the jurisdiction of the Fair Political Practices Commission. I stated that the Attorney General has primary responsibility for the enforcement of statutory provisions involving a waste of public funds and suggested that, if he wanted to obtain an opinion about the legality of such an expenditure, he should contact the Attorney General. KF:jo
embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum documenting a telephone consultation regarding a state senator's planned mailing of a letter to newspaper editors stating his position on a ballot measure, concluding that no campaign reporting obligations would apply since the letter was prepared independently by the Senator rather than under direction of a campaign committee.
